annotations.label: Radian

anchor.snippet: is the unit of angle in the International System of Units

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes angles as the quotient ℝ/2πℤ via `Real.Angle`, but does not treat the radian as a named SI unit.

annotations.label: Radian (geometric definition)

anchor.snippet: One radian is defined as the angle at the center of a circle in a plane that is subtended by an arc whose length equals the radius

annotations.note: No geometric arc-length-based definition of the radian appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Angle as ratio of arc length to radius

anchor.snippet: the magnitude in radians of a subtended angle is equal to the ratio of the arc length to the radius

annotations.note: Mathlib has no arc-length-based characterization of angle magnitude.

annotations.label: Full revolution equals 2π radians

anchor.snippet: The angle corresponding to one complete revolution is the length of the circumference divided by the radius

annotations.note: `Real.Angle.coe_two_pi` states `↑(2 * π) = (0 : Real.Angle)`, encoding that 2π radians is a full revolution.

annotations.label: Radian is dimensionless

anchor.snippet: The key fact is that the radian is a dimensionless unit equal to

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dimensional-analysis framework for SI units.

annotations.label: Pulley rise example

anchor.snippet: an object hanging by a string from a pulley will rise or drop by

annotations.note: No physics worked-example concerning a pulley is formalized.

annotations.label: Angular velocity of rolling wheel

anchor.snippet: Similarly in the formula for the angular velocity of a rolling wheel

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize physical angular-velocity formulas.

annotations.label: Capitalized Sin function (complete form)

anchor.snippet: The capitalized function Sin is the "complete" function that takes an argument with a dimension of angle

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Real.sin` takes a dimensionless real; no dimensioned-input variant is defined.

annotations.label: Converting radians to degrees

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of degrees and so no radians-to-degrees conversion.

annotations.label: Converting degrees to radians

anchor.snippet: Conversely, to convert from degrees to radians, multiply by

annotations.note: No degree unit exists in Mathlib, so this conversion is absent.

annotations.label: Converting radians to turns

anchor.snippet: Radians can be converted to turns

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `turn` unit or conversion.

annotations.label: Converting between radians and gradians

anchor.snippet: To convert from radians to gradians multiply by

annotations.note: Gradians are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Limit of sin(x)/x in radians

anchor.snippet: the use of radians leads to the simple limit formula

annotations.note: `isEquivalent_sin : sin ~[𝓝 0] id` expresses sin x ∼ x as x → 0, equivalent to sin(x)/x → 1.

annotations.label: Taylor series for sin x in radians

anchor.snippet: when x is the angle expressed in radians, the Taylor series for sin x becomes

annotations.note: `Real.sin_eq_tsum` (and `Real.hasSum_sin`) give the Taylor series for sin on ℝ.

annotations.label: Taylor series for sin in degrees

anchor.snippet: If y were the angle x but expressed in degrees

annotations.note: Mathlib has no degree-valued sine and so no degree Taylor series.

annotations.label: Angular velocity in rad/s

anchor.snippet: angular velocity is typically expressed in the unit radian per second

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define angular velocity or SI units.

annotations.label: In phase / antiphase

anchor.snippet: if the phase angle difference of two waves is

annotations.note: Phase-relation terminology for waves is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Milliradian

anchor.snippet: A milliradian (mrad) is a thousandth of a radian

annotations.note: No SI-prefix-based angle units appear in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Angular mil

anchor.snippet: The angular mil is an approximation of the milliradian

annotations.note: The angular mil is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Arc second

anchor.snippet: More common is the arc second

annotations.note: Arcseconds are not formalized in Mathlib.
